## prompt-1-mermaid

*Generated by Process A from the submitted `prompts/prompt-narrative.md`
(SUB-PROMPT SCHEDULE item 1), adapted from
`single-prompt-bill/auto-bill-02/sub-prompts/prompt-3-mermaid-selection.md` and
the curated Mermaid catalog in
`Clinical-AI-Demos/tree/main/ai-outputs/output-01`.*

Produce the curated colored Mermaid set for the H. R. 9510 narrative review into
`law-physical-ai-trials/review/mermaid/`. Deliver **21 high-quality, professional
Mermaid figures** (the schedule requires 20+), each in its own file under
`mermaid/diagrams/` and each pushed as its own commit, plus a catalog
`output-mermaid.md` that embeds all 21 for GitHub rendering and a directory
`README.md` naming the upstream files used.

**Palette (strict).** Use only black, grayscales, and the three theme colors
`#EBCB8B` (gold, hope and benefit), `#D08770` (clay, harm and risk), and
`#8B2E3F` (burgundy, the Act, verification, and human protection). This keeps the
existing `#8B2E3F` paper template theme. Do not introduce any other hue. Fills are
the theme colors or pale gray tints; strokes are black or the deep theme color;
text is black on light fills and white on the `#8B2E3F` fill. No neon, no
gradients, no cartoon rounded cards (corner radius 2 to 4 pixels only).

**Style discipline (from output-01).** Crisp near-rectangular boxes; fluent
spline connectors (`basis`, `natural`, `cardinal`, `catmullRom`, `monotoneX`,
`monotoneY`); named `classDef` node classes and container subgraphs so each
diagram scales to a more complex workflow; black text on white page background.
Use a different and most relevant Mermaid diagram type for each figure so no two
figures repeat the same pattern unnecessarily (flowchart, sequence, state,
class, journey, timeline, quadrant, mindmap, gitGraph, block, sankey, C4,
requirement, and high-complexity maps).

**Subject re-theming.** Re-theme every diagram to the narrative's subject so the
set can illustrate the eight emotional-pillar sections and the legislative
argument:

1. The verification-before-generation workflow (humans plus Claude Code and
   Codex peer review, the ten VVUQ gates, ACCEPT / ESCALATE / BLOCK).
2. The eight emotional pillars as a reader's path from compassion to urgency.
3. The bill-to-law process (introduction, committee markup, testimony and
   hearings, reconciliation, passage) and the MAIN ACTIONS (advocacy, coalition
   building, policy entrepreneurship, reconciliation).
4. The compounding-human-error chain in the legacy trial and peer-review system
   versus the verified pipeline.
5. The coalition map (patient groups, hospital networks, technology developers,
   medical boards) and the evidence-to-law lineage of the author's prior works.
6. Protection of vulnerable populations: bias surveillance, informed consent,
   and subgroup audit gates.
7. Trust scaffolding: physician endorsement, transparency, pilot results, and
   audit trails.
8. Urgency: the widening gap between technology capability and regulation over
   time.

For every figure, give a one-line title, the Mermaid diagram type, the bill
narrative slot it serves (which of the eight sections, the introduction, the
framework, or an appendix), and a one-line note on why that diagram type is
correct for that content. State that each Markdown Mermaid figure is reproduced
in the compiled LaTeX narrative as a matching **colored TikZ** figure in the same
palette, and that these figures are refined across the draft, full, and final
stages.

One commit per diagram file (21 commits), then one commit for `output-mermaid.md`
and one for `README.md`. For the second-to-last commit fix all errors across all
files; for the last commit write the directory README (repository structure,
badges, a representative colored Mermaid diagram, table of contents) and refresh
the pull request description. Commit and push in real time; do not hold commits.
Do not stall, ask questions, or go into plan mode.
